Geese are a Brooklyn band who formed when their members were still in high school, and Projector (2021) — their debut, released when they were mostly eighteen or nineteen — is either precocious in a good way or precocious in the slightly uncomfortable way, depending on how you respond to records that seem to have read more than they've lived. The art-rock ambitions are real: song structures that change time signatures and register without warning, guitar textures that move between delicacy and noise, a general willingness to sprawl.

Cameron Winter's lyrics, dense with references and affected in ways that are sometimes productive, give the record an identity that distinguishes it from mere proficiency. 3D Country (2023) found them expanding further, adding pedal steel and a broader Americana palette that suggested the sound was still in formation. Geese are a band worth watching: whether they make good on the early promise depends partly on what they do with the obvious influences they're still carrying.

artists.name: David Bowie

classicAlbums.title: The Rise and Fall of Ziggy Stardust and the Spiders from Mars
classicAlbums.classicReason: Mick Ronson's guitar — simultaneously orchestral (his string arrangements for 'Lady Stardust') and viscerally physical (the opening riff of 'Hang On to Yourself') — gives the album a range that Bowie's theatrical concept required but couldn't guarantee. 'Starman' is the moment that changed British pop culture: Bowie's Top of the Pops performance in June 1972 functions as a before/after marker for an entire generation's sense of what rock music could do or be. The concept — rock star as extraterrestrial — created the precedent for every subsequent pop persona, from Prince to Lady Gaga.

artists.bio:
David Bowie is many artists — the glam-rock alien, the blue-eyed soul singer, the Berlin experimentalist, the eighties pop icon, the nineties drum-and-bass enthusiast — and the problem with writing about him is deciding which one to begin with. The one who matters most to this graph is the Bowie of 1976–1979: the Berlin trilogy made with Brian Eno, Low, Heroes, and Lodger, records that dismantled the song's structure and reassembled it as something else. Side two of Low in particular — instrumentals named like emotional states, each one a complete thing — is one of the most radical moves a mainstream pop artist had made to that point.

But the through-line of influence is longer than the Berlin period. Ziggy Stardust (1972) defined what it meant to construct a rock persona as a formal act rather than an expression of authentic self — which is a definition with consequences for every post-punk act that followed. The production language Bowie developed with Tony Visconti, and the aesthetic logic he developed with Eno, became the grammar of alternative music in the eighties. Joy Division, the Cure, Siouxsie and the Banshees all cite him; he appears in this graph as the outside influence that explains many of the inside connections.

scenes.name: American Underground
scenes.deck: Between 1980 and 1991, a scattered network of American bands built its own infrastructure — vans, college radio, all-ages clubs, and independent labels — because the mainstream music industry offered nothing for music this loud, this strange, or this indifferent to commercial polish. It became known simply as the underground, and it rewired what American rock could sound like.

sections.heading: The Circuit
paragraphs: There was no single scene so much as a circuit: a loose, self-organized touring network stitched together by word of mouth, zines, and college radio stations willing to play what commercial rock stations would not. Bands slept on floors, split gas money, and booked their own shows in VFW halls and basements because no promoter would touch them.

Labels like SST, Twin/Tone, and Homestead ran the business side of this economy — pressing records on shoestring budgets, trading tour dates and studio time, and treating national exposure as a byproduct of persistence rather than a marketing plan. It was DIY not as an aesthetic choice but as the only available option.

sections.heading: The Bands
paragraphs: The bands that emerged from this network shared almost nothing sonically — the sludgy melodicism of Hüsker Dü, the amp-worshipping sprawl of Dinosaur Jr., Sonic Youth's detuned art-noise, the ragged tunefulness of The Replacements, R.E.M.'s jangling mystery, Pixies' loud-quiet-loud tension, Pavement's shambling irony — but they shared the circuit, the labels, and a refusal to sound like anything on the radio.
Below is the community that built it — the artists in Starweave's graph who came out of this world.

sections.heading: The Breakthrough
paragraphs: By the end of the decade, the underground's influence had outgrown its infrastructure. Nirvana — who toured the same clubs and released their debut on the same independent label system — carried the sound to the top of the charts in 1991, and major labels scrambled to sign anything that resembled it.

The circuit didn't survive its own success intact, but its DNA did: the idea that a band could build an audience without radio, without a major label, and without changing what it sounded like to get there became the founding myth of American indie rock for the next three decades.
